title: The Wingman

jokes.text: Moira clarifies that 'nothing but a little wounded pride...and one severed earlobe' resulted from the crow attack
jokes.setup: Moira reassures the morning show hosts that no one was seriously hurt in the crow attack PR stunt
jokes.punchline: She casually drops 'and one severed earlobe' as an afterthought, undermining the reassurance entirely
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira: the escalating understatement lands perfectly because she delivers it with the same breezy confidence as the non-injury disclaimer. The specificity of 'severed earlobe' is what elevates it above a generic injury joke — it's grotesque enough to be shocking but contained enough to be funny. Deeply character-integrated; only Moira would frame a severed earlobe as a minor PR footnote.

jokes.text: Moira explaining streaming: 'the brilliant thing about being on streaming is that one can start the movie at any point, any time they like'
jokes.setup: The hosts celebrate the movie trending on Interflix; Moira is invited to comment on the platform's reach
jokes.punchline: Moira's explanation of streaming reveals she barely understands it, reducing the concept to its most basic possible observation as though it's profound
jokes.explanation: This is a well-observed character joke: Moira is clearly technologically unaware but covers it with grandeur. The comedy is in how she intellectualizes something obvious. It's good character work but the punchline itself is fairly soft.

dialogue.speaker: TV Host 1
dialogue.line: We're following a developing story out of the city where a man was attacked by a swan.

dialogue.speaker: TV Host 2
dialogue.line: The incident occurred at approximately 2 PM near the waterfront...

dialogue.speaker: TV Host 1
dialogue.line: Authorities say the swan was... unprovoked.

dialogue.speaker: TV Host 2
dialogue.line: The victim is expected to make a full recovery.

jokes.text: Moira credits Alexis on live TV — 'This whole PR coup d'état never would've happened without her' — but Alexis is off-mic and can't be heard
jokes.setup: Moira makes a grand gesture of acknowledging her daughter live on morning TV
jokes.punchline: Alexis responds but Moira immediately cuts her off — 'Can't hear you. Poor dear's not mic'd' — making the acknowledgment performative rather than genuine
jokes.explanation: A compact and efficient joke about Moira's performative generosity: she offers public credit but the staging (Alexis being off-mic) makes it hollow. 'Poor dear's not mic'd' has a delicious condescension to it. Good character-specific irony.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm absolutely fine. A few bruises, nothing serious. You should see the other guy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, actually, Derek lost an earlobe, but that's hardly my concern.

jokes.text: Alexis tries to be humble and credit herself modestly, but Moira cuts her off: 'Can't hear you. Poor dear's not mic'd.'
jokes.setup: Moira grandly thanks Alexis on air; Alexis tries to say 'we just had fun with it'
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately silences her with 'Can't hear you. Poor dear's not mic'd.' — simultaneously practical and dismissive
jokes.explanation: The economy here is exceptional — 'Can't hear you. Poor dear's not mic'd' does triple duty: it's technically true, it's Moira reclaiming the spotlight, and it's delivered with a condescending 'poor dear' that reveals the real power dynamic. Only Moira could turn a mic logistics note into a character assassination.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, of course I knew it would be a smashing success. I have an impeccable eye for talent and entertainment value.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I simply chose to let others discover it organically, you understand.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Wait—it actually succeeded?

jokes.text: Bob says 'I hope that doesn't sound as sad as... as it feels' about not minding a dirty hotel room
jokes.setup: Bob is being pitched on staying at the motel; he assures them he doesn't need a clean room
jokes.punchline: He stops mid-sentence to acknowledge the sadness of what he's saying in real time, making it simultaneously funnier and more pathetic
jokes.explanation: The self-aware pause mid-sentence is beautifully structured. Bob catching himself and acknowledging the sadness of his own statement is the punchline — it's both cringe comedy and genuine pathos, which is Schitt's Creek at its best. The line is deceptively well-written.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Alexis, darling, your help with my public image has been simply divine.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Thanks, Mom. It was really just a simple social media post.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A simple post? Chérie, you have orchestrated a veritable 'tour de force' of rehabilitation! A true 'pièce de résistance!'

jokes.text: Johnny tells Bob to hydrate 'because you should really hydrate after all that crying' — Bob immediately responds 'Gwen used to love water' and sobs
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to be practical and supportive, telling Bob to grab water and hydrate after crying
jokes.punchline: Bob's brain immediately connects 'water' to Gwen, derailing any attempt at normalcy with fresh sobbing
jokes.explanation: Perfectly structured two-beat joke: Johnny's sensible advice triggers the most mundane possible Gwen association. 'Gwen used to love water' is absurdly specific and devastating. The 10-second gap before the sob (implied by the timestamp gap [03:01] to [03:11]) suggests the show lets the audience sit with it. Economy is exceptional — three words of setup pay off huge.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, she's not miked.

jokes.text: Roland's defense of having sad people at the motel: 'Johnny, there's plenty of sad people staying at this motel'
jokes.setup: Johnny says it's a bad look to have Bob wallowing around the motel
jokes.punchline: Roland responds by revealing the motel's customer base is already sad people, undermining Johnny's brand aspirations
jokes.explanation: A functional character joke that lands on Roland's blunt practicality vs. Johnny's optimism. Competent but not a standout moment.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: Yeah, so Bob's been staying with me since the divorce went through.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: He's in the spare room. Keeps asking me if I need anything, it's driving me crazy.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: I told him, 'Bob, you're not my wife anymore, you're just my roommate now.'

jokes.text: Roland's jab: 'Johnny, you know, the whole dating scene has kind of changed a little since you were single. First off, we now have electricity.'
jokes.setup: Johnny proposes acting as Bob's wingman, implying expertise in the dating world
jokes.punchline: Roland mocks Johnny's age by suggesting he was single before electricity existed
jokes.explanation: A reliable old-age joke but Roland's delivery and specificity ('First off, we now have electricity') gives it some snap. The escalating setup ('changed a little') before the absurd punchline is structurally sound. Above average but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I'm very flexible. I can work around your schedule. I'm available evenings, weekends, holidays...

jokes.text: Bob casually mentions 'lucky Gwen, she has another one of her male cousins visiting' then chuckles and immediately sobs
jokes.setup: Bob is agreeing to go out for the evening so he won't be alone
jokes.punchline: He lets slip what is clearly a reference to Gwen having an affair, disguised as an innocent comment about cousins, then the emotional whiplash of chuckling then sobbing makes it devastating
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's darkest joke and one of its best. Bob's 'another one of her male cousins' is a perfectly constructed bit of denial-comedy — he clearly knows what's going on but can't face it, and the joke lets the audience figure it out a beat before he collapses. The chuckle-then-sob rhythm is impeccably written. Quotable, rewatchable, and lands with real impact.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Would you like some water, Bob?

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Yes, please.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'll just get you a glass. I know you'll need tissues for the tears.

jokes.text: David complains that the coffee is burning his hand because he can't put it down on anything (no coffee table yet)
jokes.setup: David is enjoying the coffee Patrick made; Patrick reminds him the new handmade coffee table is coming today
jokes.punchline: David's complaint loops back to the very problem being solved — no surface to put the coffee on — showing his impatience
jokes.explanation: A light, functional setup for the Jake scene. It's cute and character-specific (David being high-maintenance) but it's essentially just exposition dressed as a joke.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Water? Well, that reminds me of the time Sheila wanted to install a koi pond. Cost me thirty thousand dollars. Thirty thousand! She said it would be 'relaxing.' You know what was relaxing? Our divorce settlement.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'You happy now? Just do me a favour and watch these, and don't eat them.' David: 'Mm. No guarantees.'
jokes.setup: The coffee table arrives just as David was suggesting Patrick sit down and relax
jokes.punchline: Patrick entrusts David with whatever he was carrying (implied food/pastries), David immediately hedges on not eating them
jokes.explanation: 'No guarantees' is a perfectly David response — simultaneously honest and irresponsible. Short, characterful, and earns its laugh through specificity of character rather than setup.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, I think 'sad people' might be a bit of a broad generalization. Perhaps we could say 'people going through a transitional period' or 'guests seeking a fresh start.'

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: They're sad people, Johnny.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, well... semantics.

jokes.text: Jake the delivery man tells Patrick 'that is a great sweater, by the way. It really brings out your lips.'
jokes.setup: Jake is delivering the coffee table; Patrick comments that Jake looks familiar
jokes.punchline: Jake's flirtatious compliment about the sweater 'bringing out your lips' is a non-sequitur that reveals his sexually forward character — he's flirting with Patrick in front of his fiancé
jokes.explanation: 'Brings out your lips' is an immediately iconic line — the deliberately absurd anatomy of the compliment (sweaters bring out eye color, not lips) signals Jake's whole deal in one line. It's funny, character-establishing, and perfectly economical. The joke does triple duty: it's funny on its own, it characterizes Jake instantly, and it sets up David's jealousy arc.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, when I was your age, we didn't have any of this swiping business. We just walked up to a woman and said, 'Hey, you look decent. Wanna grab a drink?' And if she didn't mace you, it was a yes.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Now you kids are on your little apps, matching with people. Back in my day, if a woman rejected you, at least you got to see the look of disgust on her face in real time.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You're telling me you need an algorithm to find love? When I was courting your mother, the only thing I needed was a winning smile and a willingness to lie about my job.

jokes.text: David sarcastically announces 'I am also wearing a sweater' after Jake compliments Patrick but not him
jokes.setup: Jake compliments Patrick's sweater but pointedly doesn't notice David's
jokes.punchline: David's petulant interjection reveals he's wounded by being overlooked by his ex-hookup
jokes.explanation: Pure David Rose in five words. The joke is almost entirely in what's unsaid — David's pride, his competitive streak with Patrick, his relationship with Jake — and it's a perfect character beat. The delivery of 'I am also wearing a sweater' as a flat, wounded statement is a masterclass in economy.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, you know, I'm trying to look on the bright side here. Things could be worse, right? Heh... heh... *voice breaking* ...hehehehe...

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: *sobbing* Oh my God, what am I doing? Everything is terrible.

jokes.text: Jake: 'A lot of sweat went into this one.' (referring to his own sweater, or possibly David's)
jokes.setup: David points out he is also wearing a sweater after Jake complimented Patrick's
jokes.punchline: Jake's response about sweat going into the sweater is simultaneously a deflection, possibly a double entendre, and entirely Jake — breezy and unreadable
jokes.explanation: Functional character work for Jake — his response is appropriately oblique and slightly suggestive. Works in the scene but doesn't land as a standalone joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This is actually really good coffee.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, thank you, David. I'm doing my best with what we have.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, your best is surprisingly competent. Where did you get the beans?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The general store. I've been experimenting.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, it's exceptional. Though I do have one question — where exactly am I supposed to sit while drinking it?

jokes.text: Jake's parting shot: 'And from the looks of it, so does David' (regarding good taste) — implying Patrick chose well
jokes.setup: Jake says Patrick has great taste (referencing the coffee table/sweater)
jokes.punchline: He then compliments David indirectly by crediting Patrick's taste in choosing him — another smooth, slightly uncomfortable flirtation with the couple
jokes.explanation: A smooth follow-up to the sweater joke that extends Jake's flirtatious shamelessness. Less surprising the second time but it shows Jake's consistent character — he can compliment two people at once while making both slightly uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I was just about to say something cutting about the lack of a coffee table, but—

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I know.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, that's not nearly as satisfying.

jokes.text: Patrick notices David's face is 'beet red' and 'flush' after Jake leaves; David denies it
jokes.setup: Jake has just left after a flirtatious visit
jokes.punchline: Patrick immediately calls out David's blushing, which David denies — a visual reaction beat suggesting David is more flustered by Jake than he'll admit
jokes.explanation: A functional reaction beat that transitions the Jake scene into the couple's discussion. It works because it confirms what we suspected (David is flustered) without David ever admitting it. Solid but unremarkable.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Can you just watch the pancakes for a second?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have no self-control.

jokes.text: Alexis's boyfriend Ted is on video call in 'Stewart's dorm room' because his dorm was evacuated due to fire ants, which Alexis calls 'a real Fyre Fest'
jokes.setup: Ted explains he's in a friend's dorm because his had a 'small fire ants issue'
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately riffs: 'It was a real Fyre Fest' — a pop culture pun that actually works because fire ants/Fyre Festival rhymes and both involve chaos
jokes.explanation: A clever wordplay pun that's surprisingly well-earned — fire ants becoming Fyre Fest is a legitimate if not deeply original joke. The follow-up about Krista's ankles adds texture. It's a bit of a pop culture shortcut but Alexis's breezy delivery makes it land.

dialogue.speaker: Jake
dialogue.line: Hey David, air kisses!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, that's... that's not necessary. We've moved past that.

jokes.text: Alexis about Krista's fire ant-bitten ankles: 'You do not wanna see Krista's ankles today.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is describing the fire ant infestation aftermath
jokes.punchline: The casual delivery of 'you do not wanna see Krista's ankles today' treats what sounds like a medical situation as a fashion/aesthetic problem
jokes.explanation: Alexis's delivery makes this work — treating a painful pest attack as primarily an aesthetic problem is very on-brand. 'Today' implies Krista's ankles are normally fine, which is a funny implied standard.

dialogue.speaker: Jake
dialogue.line: That sweater really hugs you in all the right places.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Okay, that's... that's inappropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Jake
dialogue.line: I'm just saying, it's a great fit.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I'm going to go now.

jokes.text: Theodore yelling 'Hi, Mrs. Rose! Congrats on the movie!' from Alexis's boyfriend's dorm room, then commenting on Moira's performance as a crow
jokes.setup: Alexis is video calling Ted; Moira appears in frame
jokes.punchline: A random dorm-mate enthusiastically weighs in on Moira's crow film performance with unexpected critical detail — 'that last scene where you leap from your nest only to discover your wings aren't developed enough'
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the absurdity of a random college student having deeply specific opinions about The Crowening, which underscores the film's bizarre viral success. Moira's gracious reception of the praise is a nice secondary beat. Solid but not standout.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I got a haircut.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Nice.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I also learned to make hollandaise sauce from scratch.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's great.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: And I've been going to the gym.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Good for you.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I'm also fluent in French now.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wow.

jokes.text: Moira reacts to Theodore's compliment: 'The Daily Mail called it a flap for the ages!'
jokes.setup: Theodore compliments Moira's crow performance
jokes.punchline: 'A flap for the ages' is simultaneously a crow-related pun (flapping wings) and a tabloid-style hyperbolic headline, delivered by Moira with complete self-satisfaction
jokes.explanation: A tightly constructed pun that works on two levels simultaneously — crow vocabulary and tabloid hyperbole — and is perfectly in-character for Moira to quote with pride. Elegant economy.

dialogue.speaker: David Rose
dialogue.line: I take great pride in my work. Every piece is handled with care, attention to detail, and a certain... firmness of grip.

dialogue.speaker: Moira Rose
dialogue.line: David, that's what she said.

jokes.text: Roland's insult about Johnny's suit on Bob: 'you look like a guy who had a million bucks, but then lost it'
jokes.setup: Johnny says Bob looks like 'a million bucks' in his borrowed suit
jokes.punchline: Roland refines the compliment to capture Bob's actual situation — a man who once had wealth but has lost everything — which is accidentally more accurate than funny
jokes.explanation: A competent callback-style riff on Johnny's setup line. Roland's clarification is both a dig at Johnny's optimism and accidentally a truthful description of Bob's circumstances, giving it some unexpected weight. Good structure.

jokes.text: Ronnie cuts off Johnny's attempt to speak on behalf of women with a flat 'You can't.'
jokes.setup: Johnny begins to caveat: 'I don't know if I can speak on behalf of women here—'
jokes.punchline: Ronnie immediately and flatly confirms: 'You can't.'
jokes.explanation: Two words. Ronnie's deadpan is her defining characteristic and this is a perfect deployment of it. The joke works because Johnny's self-awareness is immediately validated and dismissed in the same beat. Economy score of 9.5 — you cannot do more with less.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm fine. Completely fine. Jake doesn't affect me at all.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, you're literally sweating through your shirt.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's warm in here.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: We're outside.

jokes.text: When Johnny shares Moira's dating wisdom ('she was always attracted to men who could make her laugh'), Roland replies 'How'd she end up with you?'
jokes.setup: Johnny invokes Moira's advice about humor being attractive
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately implies Johnny isn't funny, questioning how Moira ended up with him
jokes.explanation: A classic roast-format retort that lands because of Roland's delivery and the rhythm of the exchange. The setup is unusually good for this type of joke (Johnny inadvertently setting himself up), but the punchline itself is fairly conventional.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, the fire ants have completely taken over the town. It's like Folderol all over again.

jokes.text: Roland's mock-female voice: 'Hello, I'm... I'm Gwen.' (choosing Gwen's name in the bar roleplay)
jokes.setup: The group is doing a mock bar scenario where Roland plays 'the woman'; Bob is supposed to practice his pickup approach
jokes.punchline: Roland, playing a random woman, instinctively chooses the name 'Gwen' — Bob's estranged wife — because 'they're always together'
jokes.explanation: Beautifully constructed cringe comedy: the whole group is trying to help Bob practice moving on, and Roland accidentally picks the worst possible name. His justification ('they're always together') is simultaneously sweet and devastating. The joke builds over several lines as Bob's reaction escalates. Rewatch value because you can see it coming once you know Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so you're saying that turtle eggs are actually a delicacy? Because I would totally eat those.

jokes.text: Johnny pivots from 'Gwen' by switching to 'Hi, I'm Ronnie. That's my friend Gwen.'
jokes.setup: Johnny takes over the roleplay from Roland to correct the 'Gwen' mistake
jokes.punchline: In trying to fix the error, Johnny still references 'Gwen' as a background character, prolonging Bob's agony
jokes.explanation: A perfect escalation of the Gwen gag — Johnny tries to be helpful but makes the exact same mistake again. The escalation structure (mistake → correction that repeats mistake) is classic farce. Gets a big laugh because the audience is already primed.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The review says, 'Ms. Rose's performance was absolutely soaring.' I'm a bird!

jokes.text: Ronnie announces she's going to 'go mingle' and 'might not come back' from the wingman mission
jokes.setup: The roleplay is collapsing; Bob is crying; the whole wingman plan is imploding
jokes.punchline: Ronnie's flat, practical exit announcement — delivered with zero drama — is the deadpan contrast to the emotional chaos around her
jokes.explanation: Ronnie's character is defined by pragmatic detachment from chaos, and this exit line captures it perfectly. 'I might not come back' said with complete calm while Bob cries is a strong character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You look like a million bucks.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: A million bucks? More like a million bucks in debt.

jokes.text: Patrick tells David 'this coffee table really brings out your lips' — directly quoting Jake's earlier line
jokes.setup: David and Patrick are admiring the newly arrived coffee table
jokes.punchline: Patrick uses Jake's exact flirtatious line to tease David, turning it into a gentle jab about Jake's earlier compliment
jokes.explanation: A brilliant callback that elevates the original joke. Patrick using Jake's absurd line to tease David is a perfect relationship moment — it shows Patrick is secure enough to joke about it while also needling David. The callback lands harder than the original because it recontextualizes Jake's line as something both Patrick and the audience found ridiculous. High quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, that explains a lot, doesn't it?

jokes.text: David: 'Not in my experience.' (when Patrick says Jake is a 'one-man operation')
jokes.setup: Patrick says Jake is impressive as 'a one-man operation' (referring to furniture making)
jokes.punchline: David's 'Not in my experience' confirms Jake is emphatically NOT a one-man operation in other respects — a revealing overshare delivered completely deadpan
jokes.explanation: An incredibly economical innuendo that works because David delivers it as a calm factual correction without any awareness of how revealing it is. Three words that confirm David has first-hand knowledge of Jake's social operations. The audience gets the implication before Patrick does, which is perfect comedic positioning. Only David Rose would say this this way.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Okay, so you approach her like this... 'Hi, I'm Johnny Rose, I've made some unfortunate business decisions, but I have excellent posture.'

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: That's not really—

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, no, let me finish. Then you say, 'Sheila, I know we had our differences, but I think we could really make this work.'

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa—that's my ex-wife's name!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Is it? Well, that explains the look on your face.

jokes.text: David explaining Jake: 'Jake is nobody's ex and everybody's ex'
jokes.setup: Patrick says he's fine having drinks with 'one of David's exes' (Jake)
jokes.punchline: David corrects him with this perfectly compact description of Jake's relationship philosophy
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best line. 'Jake is nobody's ex and everybody's ex' is a genuinely clever, paradoxical formulation that tells you everything about Jake's character in 10 words. It's original, economical, perfectly structured as a chiasmus, and could only come from David's particular brand of jaded self-awareness. This is what 8+ looks like: instantly quotable, structurally elegant, and character-defining.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I wanted something that would set him apart. Something memorable. David is a very common name, so I thought, 'Why not make it interesting?' I named him David because it's a solid, dependable name. Like a good investment portfolio.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That's lovely, dear.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: The middle name, Rose, was your mother's idea. I wanted to honor that. Though I'll admit, I didn't think through how it would sound with the last name.

jokes.text: David's warning: 'going to Jake's for a drink is never just going to Jake's for a drink'
jokes.setup: Patrick suggests they could just go have a drink at Jake's without it meaning anything
jokes.punchline: David establishes that Jake's invitations are inherently loaded — 'a whiskey or whatever' always means something more
jokes.explanation: A solid character joke that builds on Jake's established persona. The repetition structure ('never just going to Jake's for a drink') is clean and effective. It's also doing plot work, which makes it slightly less pure as a joke.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Bob, listen to me. I can see it now. You're going to be very successful. Very successful indeed.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes. In about ten years, you're going to own your own business.

jokes.text: David's concession: 'if we ever did decide to have a whiskey with someone, Jake would be the person to do that with because he literally doesn't care about anything'
jokes.setup: David has just warned against going to Jake's; then immediately starts arguing the case for going
jokes.punchline: David's justification for Jake — 'he literally doesn't care about anything' — is a darkly funny endorsement that says everything and nothing
jokes.explanation: David talking himself into something he just talked himself out of is a reliable character beat, and 'he literally doesn't care about anything' as a selling point for a sexual encounter is a great dark joke. The qualifier 'I'm just throwing it out there' makes it even better.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You know, this is a really nice coffee table.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Thank you. It's reclaimed wood from a 19th-century French château.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: It really brings out the color of your eyes.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: ...Did you just compliment my eyes by referencing furniture?

jokes.text: David's shopping cart analogy: 'I always have the option of online shopping and just putting things in the cart. That's not gonna happen.'
jokes.setup: Patrick says they could always just go and have 'a whiskey' (implying they wouldn't take it further)
jokes.punchline: David counters with an analogy about online shopping and adding to cart — acknowledging the temptation while predicting they'd inevitably follow through
jokes.explanation: A beautifully David Rose analogy — turning a sexual temptation discussion into an e-commerce metaphor is both unexpected and perfectly in character. 'That's not gonna happen' delivered with resigned certainty is the ideal button. High character integration: this analogy could only come from David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Jake is in a relationship, but he's also seeing other people. It's very confusing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: He's simultaneously committed and completely unavailable.

jokes.text: Moira mentions the movie is tied with 'the fourth season of Caroline in the City' on Interflix
jokes.setup: Moira is celebrating being Interflix's number one stream of the day
jokes.punchline: The triumph is immediately deflated by the reveal that they're tied with the fourth season of a forgotten '90s sitcom
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'fourth season' is key — not the popular early seasons but the declining fourth season of a mid-tier sitcom. It's a well-calibrated insult to the film's cultural reach. 'Caroline in the City' is a real show, which makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Look, I understand the appeal, but I have to exercise some self-control here. It's like... I'm Odysseus, and temptation is the siren song, and I am simply choosing to tie myself to the mast.

jokes.text: Moira distinguishing the flowers: 'They're all for you. Not those — those are from Joyce DeWitt.'
jokes.setup: A room full of flower arrangements; Moira seems to be gesturing Alexis gets all of them
jokes.punchline: She casually clarifies that a subset are from Joyce DeWitt (Janet from Three's Company), implying Moira has famous friends who send her flowers
jokes.explanation: Moira's casual celebrity name-drop (Joyce DeWitt specifically, not a huge star) perfectly characterizes her world — she moves in circles that include D-list celebrities who still remember her. The specificity elevates it above a generic name-drop.

jokes.text: Moira's 'advice': after describing Alexis's career opportunity at length, she says 'And on the other hand?' — Alexis says 'Ted!' — Moira gasps and says 'Dear Ted. Yes, this is a quandary.'
jokes.setup: Moira has delivered a long, eloquent speech about Alexis's career opportunity. Alexis asks 'And on the other hand?' expecting balance
jokes.punchline: Moira had no 'other hand' — she forgot Ted entirely — and only remembers him when Alexis says his name, at which point she treats him as an afterthought with 'Dear Ted. Yes, this is a quandary.'
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed joke that's also character-revealing: Moira gives a lush, theatrical speech about career with no mention of Ted, and the joke is that she simply didn't have an 'other hand.' Her recovery — the gasp, 'Dear Ted. Yes, this is a quandary.' — is both funny and emotionally telling. The structure of the joke mirrors Moira's priorities perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: These are for Alexis. She's going through a phase where she only accepts flowers that have been hand-selected by someone with impeccable taste and a working knowledge of obscure 1970s television actresses.

jokes.text: Bob's attempt at bar socializing results in running into his marriage counselor, who tells him she can't keep seeing him because she chose Gwen in the divorce
jokes.setup: The wingman group watches Bob approach a woman at the bar; it looks like it's going well (hugging, etc.)
jokes.punchline: Bob reveals it was his marriage counselor, who has now formally taken Gwen's side in the divorce — a darkly absurd new low
jokes.explanation: A genuinely surprising dark comedy beat: the apparent success (hugging!) is immediately deflated to reveal a new, absurd humiliation. The marriage counselor 'choosing Gwen' is both tragic and hilarious — it's an entirely original comedic scenario that escalates Bob's situation in a fresh direction. The setup (watching from afar, mistaking it for success) is well-constructed misdirection.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I just... I've never gotten flowers from a professional before.

jokes.text: Johnny proposes the group laugh on a count of three as if Bob told a very funny joke — the fake group laugh strategy
jokes.setup: After the marriage counselor disaster, the group needs a new strategy
jokes.punchline: Johnny's elaborate plan is to laugh performatively, then they actually do it — and it fails immediately
jokes.explanation: The plan itself is a good comic premise — a fake laugh as social engineering. What makes it work is the escalation into reality and immediate failure. Bob's 'What if they ask me what I said?' is the right detail to undermine the plan before it's executed.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm so worried about the flowers. What if they don't survive the trip?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, some flowers are simply meant to wilt.

jokes.text: Johnny's plan to attract attention: 'We all laugh on the count of three, as if Bob just told a very funny joke'
jokes.setup: All conventional approaches have failed; Bob can't talk to anyone
jokes.punchline: Johnny's social strategy is essentially to fake group happiness at nothing — a desperately optimistic plan that also characterizes Johnny as a man who genuinely believes this will work
jokes.explanation: The plan itself is the joke — it's both recognizable as a real bar strategy and completely doomed. Johnny's earnestness in presenting it as viable is the heart of the comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, darling, you simply cannot pass up an opportunity like this. A chance to expand your horizons, to reinvent yourself in a new city. It's what I did, and look at me now.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, I have a boyfriend.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, well, these things are fleeting, aren't they?

jokes.text: The fake group laughing, performed badly enough that a woman thinks they're laughing AT her
jokes.setup: Johnny's plan: fake-laugh in sync to attract a woman's attention
jokes.punchline: The laugh is so artificial that the nearest woman thinks she's being mocked, angrily confronting them
jokes.explanation: Classic sitcom execution: the obvious failure of an already-doomed plan. The impact is strong because it's the inevitable consequence played out in the worst possible way. Roland's badly-suppressed snort into the confrontation makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Things are really turning around for me at the bar. I've been working on my confidence, my social skills...

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: In fact, I picked someone up last night.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You did?

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Yes. We went back to her place, we talked for hours...

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Bob, that's amazing!

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, it turns out it was my therapist. She was just... listening to me.

jokes.text: Bob's aside during the confrontation: 'You said they wouldn't ask' (after the woman demands to know what was funny)
jokes.setup: The angry woman demands to know what Bob said that was so funny
jokes.punchline: Bob immediately throws Johnny under the bus by citing his earlier assurance that no one would ask
jokes.explanation: A tight, well-timed callback. Bob's timing in delivering 'you said they wouldn't ask' at exactly the moment Johnny is proven wrong is structurally perfect. Five words, maximum impact.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Even my therapist is choosing my ex-wife over me.

jokes.text: The angry woman's exit line: 'Well, next time, observe someone else.'
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to cover by saying 'it was just a funny observation'
jokes.punchline: The woman shoots back 'next time, observe someone else' — a clever wordplay retort on 'observation'
jokes.explanation: A quick, sharp retort that plays on 'observation' — economical wordplay delivered as a walk-off line. The character has one line and makes it count. Strong structure, very economical.

dialogue.speaker: Woman
dialogue.line: What did you say that was funny?

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I... Johnny said I was funny earlier!

jokes.text: Roland's sound effect after the failed approach: 'Crrrash and burn, Johnny.'
jokes.setup: The woman has walked away; Johnny admits he 'buckled under the pressure'
jokes.punchline: Roland provides his sound-effect commentary — 'Crrrash and burn' — with gleeful relish
jokes.explanation: Roland as the gleeful commentator on failure is consistent characterization but the joke itself is fairly conventional. The sound effect delivery is the best part.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny Rose
dialogue.line: It was actually Bob's observation.

dialogue.speaker: Woman at bar
dialogue.line: Well, Bob's an idiot.

jokes.text: Johnny's excuse: 'She was a dead ringer for Gwen.'
jokes.setup: Johnny explains why he froze when the woman confronted them
jokes.punchline: He blames his failure on the woman looking like Gwen — reintroducing Gwen as the specter haunting the entire evening
jokes.explanation: A solid callback to the Gwen running gag that also characterizes Johnny's anxiety in the moment. The fact that Gwen is now haunting random strangers' faces shows how saturated the evening has been with her presence.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You miss 100% of the shots you don't take. Wayne Gretzky. Michael Scott.

jokes.text: David's shirt confrontation: Patrick's 'Oh, didn't know we were bringing our friends tonight' about David's shirt
jokes.setup: David arrives at Patrick's car in a new shirt to go to Jake's
jokes.punchline: Patrick immediately mocks the shirt as if it's a separate entity — 'our friends tonight' — implying the shirt is so large/loud it counts as additional guests
jokes.explanation: A well-structured fashion insult that's specific enough to be funny without just being generic mockery. The 'bringing our friends' framing is a clever conceit.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Everything reminds me of my ex-wife.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'Is it new?' David: 'No...I just haven't worn it yet.' Patrick: 'So it is new then.'
jokes.setup: Patrick asks if David's shirt is new; David defensively says no, then admits he hasn't worn it
jokes.punchline: Patrick's logical trap — 'So it is new then' — catches David's evasion perfectly
jokes.explanation: A tightly constructed logical trap delivered with Patrick's characteristic dry precision. The joke works because David's distinction ('not new, just unworn') is both recognizable vanity logic and completely absurd when exposed. Very economical — the whole exchange runs about 15 words.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That shirt really... hugs you in all the right places.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'Is that cologne I smell?' David: 'No. This is a leather-scented body moisturizer.' Patrick: 'So, you moisturized your body then.'
jokes.setup: Patrick sniffs and asks about cologne; David denies wearing cologne but reveals something functionally equivalent
jokes.punchline: Patrick applies the same logical trap he just used with the shirt: the distinction David draws (moisturizer vs. cologne) is meaningless in practice
jokes.explanation: This joke is better than the shirt version because 'leather-scented body moisturizer' is inherently funnier than 'I haven't worn it yet' — it's more specific, more David, and more absurd. The repeated logical trap structure works as an escalating running gag. Patrick's 'So, you moisturized your body then' lands harder because we've seen the pattern once. The specificity of 'leather-scented' is exceptional character work.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: So what cologne do you wear?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't wear cologne. I use a custom scent that's been developed specifically for my pH balance and natural musk. It's a blend of Bulgarian rose, Tahitian vanilla, and a hint of vetiver that I have imported quarterly from a small apothecary in Grasse.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Right. So... cologne.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No. It's a *bespoke fragrance experience*. I also have a separate morning and evening iteration, depending on my circadian rhythm and the ambient humidity levels.

jokes.text: Patrick's fashion advice: 'The only thing that's complicated here is the length of your sleeves. Go long, go short. Just pick one.'
jokes.setup: David suggests they 'not do this' because things are getting complicated
jokes.punchline: Patrick redirects: the only actual complication is David's sleeve length — a perfect practical observation about indeterminate sleeve length while also dismissing David's overthinking
jokes.explanation: A funny, specific fashion observation that also functions as character comedy — Patrick is grounded and practical while David agonizes. 'Go long, go short. Just pick one.' has good rhythm.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, the sleeves are fine. They're a quarter inch shorter than the other jacket.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: But is that intentional?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Does it matter? It's a shirt.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: It matters to me.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, it shouldn't. You look fine. You look better than fine, you look great. Why do you do this?

jokes.text: Patrick: 'A lot of people are here for drinks, David.' — The 'drinks' gathering is actually a crowded party with a massage circle
jokes.setup: Jake invited them for 'drinks'; they arrive to find a full party
jokes.punchline: Patrick's deadpan 'a lot of people are here for drinks' acknowledges the obvious gap between the invitation and reality
jokes.explanation: Patrick's dry acknowledgment that David was completely right is well-delivered. The callback to David's earlier warning lands because Patrick's delivery admits his error without saying so. Efficient.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, this is... a lot of people in a very small space.

jokes.text: Jake: 'We're gonna start the massage circle soon. Don't wanna miss it.'
jokes.setup: David and Patrick are trying to leave using the 'kosher wine in the car' excuse
jokes.punchline: Jake's casual mention of the massage circle — said with zero awareness that this is alarming — is perfectly in character and makes their exit more urgent
jokes.explanation: Jake's massage circle announcement is one of the episode's funniest moments because of how casually he says it — as if everyone hosts mass massage circles. The character integration is near-perfect: this line is completely impossible without Jake's established shamelessness. 'Don't wanna miss it' treats their attendance as a given, which is the funniest possible framing.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I need to go to the car.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I left my emotional support wine out there.

jokes.text: David and Patrick's exit excuse: 'We left our wine in the car... it's kosher.'
jokes.setup: Jake says not to worry about the wine, there's plenty
jokes.punchline: David escalates to 'it's kosher' as an unanswerable reason they must retrieve their specific wine — a completely made-up urgency that signals their panic
jokes.explanation: A solid desperate-excuse joke. The specificity of 'kosher' as the unanswerable trump card is a recognizable social maneuver elevated by David's delivery.

dialogue.speaker: David Rose
dialogue.line: Well, I've planned a very special evening for us.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick Brewer
dialogue.line: Oh? What did you have in mind?

dialogue.speaker: David Rose
dialogue.line: I was thinking we could... reorganize the bedroom closet.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick Brewer
dialogue.line: David, that's the least sexy thing you've ever said to me.

dialogue.speaker: David Rose
dialogue.line: I know. Isn't it thrilling?

jokes.text: Stevie is at Jake's party — 'Can I not have one thing for myself?'
jokes.setup: David and Patrick discover Stevie at Jake's party as they're trying to leave
jokes.punchline: Stevie's indignant 'can I not have one thing for myself?' frames her attendance at Jake's orgy-adjacent party as a matter of personal privacy
jokes.explanation: Stevie's indignation is perfect: she's caught at a Jake party and rather than being embarrassed, she's offended that David and Patrick are there. 'Can I not have one thing for myself?' is a great line that implies she's a Jake regular and David has now invaded even this corner of her life. Strong character integration — this is completely Stevie.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, what are you two doing here? I'm trying to have fun for once in my life.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We're just... we came to support you.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, you're not supporting me. You're suffocating me. There's a difference.

jokes.text: Stevie deciding not to leave with David and Patrick: 'I still have like... half a drink left, so I think I'm gonna finish it here, and then... meet you guys later?' followed by 'No, I won't.'
jokes.setup: David asks if Stevie is coming with them; she starts to give a reasonable excuse
jokes.punchline: She immediately and honestly corrects herself — 'No, I won't' — admitting she has no intention of leaving
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes of the episode. Stevie starts giving a socially acceptable excuse and then abandons it mid-sentence to tell the truth. 'No, I won't' is completely Stevie — her deadpan honesty over social niceties. The rhythm of the self-correction is brilliantly timed and very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: The turtle eggs are really important for conservation.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, Ted, I don't care about the eggs. I just think it's so sweet that you're protecting them.

jokes.text: David: 'We came here to say hi and now we're leaving.' / Stevie (deadpan): 'Mm-hmm.' / David: 'Can I not have one thing for myself?'
jokes.setup: David is caught at Jake's party and tries to pretend there's an innocent explanation
jokes.punchline: Stevie's 'Mm-hmm' says everything without saying anything; then David's 'Can I not have one thing for myself?' is a hilariously self-pitying complaint given the context
jokes.explanation: Stevie's silence does more work than any line could. David's 'Can I not have one thing for myself?' is funny because 'one thing' is a very loaded description of what he came here for. Good character economy.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Ted, your apartment renovation is amazing!

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, I just painted the walls and replaced some outlets.

jokes.text: Ted apologizes for being late because 'Myrtle was laying her eggs and the whole team was really excited'; Alexis says 'Mmm! Yeah, I bet. Fresh omelettes.'
jokes.setup: Ted excitedly explains the sea turtle egg-laying event that delayed him
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately and cheerfully interprets sea turtle eggs as a food opportunity — 'Fresh omelettes' — revealing her complete ignorance of conservation
jokes.explanation: Classic Alexis — cheerful, well-meaning, and completely wrong. 'Fresh omelettes' said with genuine enthusiasm is the kind of oblivious character-specific joke Schitt's Creek does best. Ted's gentle correction about ecosystem disruption and cholesterol is a perfect double-take.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I've decided to take matters into my own hands and really spruce up my place.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I'm going to paint the walls, maybe install some new shelving.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: How hard could it be?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, turns out I'm not a contractor. I'm a veterinarian.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: My walls now look like a Jackson Pollock painting, if Jackson Pollock hated aesthetics.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: The shelving fell down and took out my TV.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: And I'm pretty sure there's a gas leak now.

jokes.text: Ted's deadpan correction: 'Well, Myrtle's a green sea turtle, and they only spawn every two years, so eating her eggs would actually disrupt an entire ecosystem. Plus, there's like a ton of cholesterol.'
jokes.setup: Alexis cheerfully suggests the turtle eggs would make omelettes
jokes.punchline: Ted's response escalates from ecological concern (serious) to cholesterol warning (mundane) — the juxtaposition of ecosystem disruption with 'a ton of cholesterol' is the joke
jokes.explanation: Ted's correction does something clever: it takes Alexis's joke seriously enough to rebut it ecologically, then deflates with a health concern. 'Plus, there's like a ton of cholesterol' treating dietary concerns as equivalent to environmental ones is a subtle, very funny escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: A shared bathroom? That would have been a dealbreaker for me.

jokes.text: Ted admits the screens he installed are 'actually attracting more bugs somehow'
jokes.setup: Ted explains why Alexis can't visit — he's made the apartment worse despite renovating for her
jokes.punchline: His lovingly installed window screens have made the bug problem worse — a pathetically funny detail that underscores his inability to prepare the place
jokes.explanation: A nicely specific detail that earns its laugh through precise absurdity — screens attracting bugs is both plausible and pathetic. The 'somehow' is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Oh, this is nice. Finally, we get some private time together.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know, I've been looking forward to this all day.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: So, should we...?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yes, let's just—

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Ow! Ow! Oh my God!

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What? What happened?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Fire ants! There are fire ants all over me!

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I don't know how they got here but—ow, ow, ow—this is not romantic!

jokes.text: Ted: 'Sorry. I can't tell if the screen's frozen, or if you're just really shocked.'
jokes.setup: Ted has just delivered the emotional news that he doesn't think Alexis should come; Alexis goes silent
jokes.punchline: Ted reads her silence as either technical difficulty or emotional shock, giving equal weight to both — a gentle, characteristically Ted way of lightening a heavy moment
jokes.explanation: A lovely little joke that does emotional heavy lifting: it relieves tension after a serious moment while characterizing Ted as gentle and self-deprecating. The tech/emotion equivalence is a uniquely modern joke that lands in a realistic way.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I appreciate the vote of confidence, but I really don't want to get into the car business.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why not? You clearly know what you're doing.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: That's exactly why I don't want to do it. I've spent my entire life avoiding the one thing I'm actually competent at.

jokes.text: Alexis suggests that maybe Ted leading with the shared bathroom situation 'could've saved us some time'
jokes.setup: Ted mentions there's also a shared bathroom situation at his place
jokes.punchline: Alexis's practical response — the bathroom might have been the dealbreaker all along — cuts through the emotional conversation with her priorities clearly displayed
jokes.explanation: A perfectly Alexis joke: in the middle of a tender, significant relationship moment, she reduces everything to the shared bathroom. The delivery presumably keeps it charming rather than cruel. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: A closer? I thought you said you were a 'clozer,' like someone who closes their eyes a lot.

jokes.text: Alexis pivots from the breakup talk to phone sex: 'Nobody's home, so why don't we start by teleporting you out of that shirt?'
jokes.setup: Alexis and Ted have agreed they'll 'wait it out'; Ted jokes about teleportation
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately seizes 'teleportation' as a segue to suggest phone intimacy — pivoting from emotional conversation to flirtation with characteristic speed
jokes.explanation: A fun character moment that shows Alexis's resilience and flirtatious nature even in emotional moments. The teleportation callback is smooth.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I'd close out my tab, but I'm not sure the account can handle that kind of emotional trauma.

jokes.text: Ted's response to Alexis's phone sex invitation: 'That's not really how teleportation works, but I think that I can--' then immediately interrupted by fire ants
jokes.setup: Alexis has proposed starting the teleportation metaphor; Ted is about to agree
jokes.punchline: Ted's literal-minded scientific correction of the teleportation metaphor ('that's not really how teleportation works') followed by immediate willingness is a perfect Ted beat, then the fire ants return at the worst possible moment
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured joke: Ted's scientific pedantry about teleportation while agreeing to the premise is classic character comedy, and then the fire ants return as a running gag callback at exactly the most inconvenient moment. The escalation from emotional conversation → flirtation → interrupted by fire ants is a perfect comic rhythm.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm not setting you up with anyone else, Roland. I'm done.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh come on, Johnny. One more time. What about that woman at the real estate office? The one with the... you know, the face?

jokes.text: Bob returning from his successful approach, Roland greeting him with 'Welcome to the players' club'
jokes.setup: Bob returns on a motorcycle, having connected with a woman
jokes.punchline: Roland's 'Welcome to the players' club' is an absurd, dated framing for the modest achievement of Bob talking to someone — immediately punctured by Bob rejecting the high-five
jokes.explanation: The joke is more in Bob's 'I'm not high-fiving that' refusal than Roland's line. Roland's cliché is the setup; Bob's dignity the punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The wine tasting notes describe a full-bodied experience with a long, lingering finish that coats the palate and leaves you completely satisfied.

jokes.text: Bob: 'No, I'm not high-fiving that.'
jokes.setup: Roland offers a high-five to celebrate Bob's success
jokes.punchline: Bob refuses with complete composure — 'I'm not high-fiving that' — maintaining his dignity amid the absurdity of the situation
jokes.explanation: Clean, simple, economical. Bob's refusal of the high-five shows his self-awareness even in a positive moment — he knows the situation doesn't warrant a 'players' club' celebration. Six words, exactly right.

jokes.text: Roland on Johnny's dating history: 'That was probably because women used to close out their tabs and go home when you came into the bar.'
jokes.setup: Johnny claims he used to be called 'the closer' in his single days
jokes.punchline: Roland recontextualizes 'the closer' — not as a pickup artist, but as the person whose arrival caused women to leave
jokes.explanation: A genuinely clever recontextualization joke: Roland takes Johnny's boast and flips the meaning of 'closer' completely. The joke is structurally sound (setup the boast → redefine the term → it's actually an insult), the wordplay is original, and it's excellently economical. One of Roland's best lines in the episode.

jokes.text: Roland's escalating comeback: 'Which is what the women used to say to each other when they saw you walking through the door.' / Johnny: 'I'm not setting you up anymore, Roland.' / Roland: 'Which is what your matchmaker said!'
jokes.setup: Johnny says 'speaking of closing out tabs, we should get out of here'
jokes.punchline: Roland extends the 'closing' insult further, then when Johnny says he won't 'set Roland up' anymore, Roland immediately applies that as another insult — 'which is what your matchmaker said' — extending the game to a third beat
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured escalating exchange. Roland has identified a structural game (taking anything Johnny says and turning it into an insult) and executes it three times, with the third beat ('which is what your matchmaker said!') landing hardest because it recontextualizes Johnny's own words as the setup. The structure score reflects how elegantly Roland chains these.

jokes.text: The wine label copy: 'Who is she and how do we get a mouth full of her?'
jokes.setup: A fruit vintner wants to make a Moira rosé as an exclusive release; Moira reads the proposed label copy or pitch
jokes.punchline: 'Who is she and how do we get a mouth full of her?' is a deliberately suggestive wine description that Moira delivers with complete unselfconsciousness
jokes.explanation: The joke is Moira's complete comfort with this suggestive wine copy — she reads it with the same enthusiasm as any other compliment. It's a great closing button for the episode's Moira arc, and 'how do we get a mouth full of her?' is genuinely memorable.

tmdb_overview: Alexis and Moira deal with the aftermath of a viral video, while Johnny helps Bob get back out in the dating world.
insight: Cringe-driven character comedy sustains a below-average episode with minimal standout moments.
